---
title: "Hands-on Factor Models: from PCA to Latent Factors in the S&P 500"
date: "2026-07-06"
description: "PCA and factor analysis on 89 S&P 500 stocks' daily returns in Python — letting the data name its own common factors: tech, energy, defensives."
categories: [causalml, factor-models, pca, python]
draft: true
bibliography: ../../references.bib
csl: ../../apa.csl
---

*Code (original R + Python rewrite): [github.com/Mullerchen910912/factor-models-in-r](https://github.com/Mullerchen910912/factor-models-in-r)*

---

## A few forces drive many series

A factor model says that many observed series are really driven by a **few
unobserved common factors**. Eighty-nine stocks move together every day because
of a handful of forces — "the market," "sector rotation." This post does it by
hand on S&P 500 returns and lets the data answer two questions: **how many
factors, and what are they?**

## 1. Prices to a returns matrix

89 constituents, 2013–2022 daily adjusted prices. Compute returns, pivot to a
`date × stock` matrix:

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("sp500_prices.csv", usecols=["symbol", "date", "adjusted"])
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
df = df.sort_values(["symbol", "date"])
df["ret"] = df.groupby("symbol")["adjusted"].pct_change()
wide = df.pivot(index="date", columns="symbol", values="ret").dropna()
# 2,384 days × 89 stocks
```

## 2. How many factors? PCA

```python
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

X = StandardScaler().fit_transform(wide.values)
pca = PCA().fit(X)
evr = pca.explained_variance_ratio_
print(evr[0], evr[:3].sum(), evr[:5].sum())
```

**PC1 alone explains 40.8%** of the variance (the "market" factor); the top 3
explain 50.8% and the top 5 explain 55.2%. A handful of factors capture most of
89 stocks' co-movement — the whole point of a factor model.

![Scree plot](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Mullerchen910912/factor-models-in-r/main/python/figures/scree.png)

## 3. What are the factors? Varimax + sector labels

PCA gives the *number*; to read *meaning* we rotate. Fit a 3-factor varimax model,
then tag each factor's top-loading stocks with their **sector** — let the data
name itself:

```python
from sklearn.decomposition import FactorAnalysis

fa = FactorAnalysis(n_components=3, rotation="varimax").fit(X)
loadings = pd.DataFrame(fa.components_.T, index=wide.columns,
                        columns=["Factor1", "Factor2", "Factor3"])
```

The three factors come out remarkably clean:

| Factor | Top-loading stocks | Sector (from data) |
|---|---|---|
| **Factor 1** | ADBE, SNPS, MSFT, NVDA, GOOGL | Information Technology |
| **Factor 2** | COP, CVX, XOM, EOG, PXD | Energy |
| **Factor 3** | DUK, D, PEP, NEE, PG, KO | Utilities / Consumer Staples (defensives) |

We gave the model no sector information, yet it recovered "tech, energy,
defensives" on its own.

## 4. One step toward causal inference

The same factor structure underpins modern causal inference. Bai & Wang's (2024)
**causal factor model** [@baiwang2024] uses "absorb common shocks with factors" to
estimate treatment effects — the same thread as my posts on
[IFE / CCE panel estimation](ife-vs-cce-panel.qmd) and
[policy targeting](policy-targeting-uplift.qmd). The repo includes the Bai-Wang R
walkthrough too.

## Takeaways

| Step | Tool | Question |
|---|---|---|
| Wrangling | pandas | prices → returns matrix |
| How many factors | PCA (sklearn) | dimension of co-movement |
| What are they | FactorAnalysis + varimax | economic meaning |
| Toward causal | Bai-Wang causal factor model | effects under factors |

Statistical factor models are the basic skill of "finding common structure
nonparametrically"; see Tsay (2010) ch. 9 [@tsay2010]. Full code:
[**factor-models-in-r**](https://github.com/Mullerchen910912/factor-models-in-r).

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
